title: Canelés de Bordeaux
subtitle: Bordeaux's caramelised rum-and-vanilla custard cakes in fluted moulds
excerpt: The iconic small cake from Bordeaux: a deeply caramelised, almost lacquered exterior with a soft, custardy, rum-and-vanilla interior. Made in copper moulds, baked at high heat. The batter rests overnight before baking.
sourceNotes: Canelés de Bordeaux are attributed to the nuns of the Couvent des Annonciades in Bordeaux, with the current form standardised by the mid-nineteenth century. The recipe was published in French household cooking guides of the late nineteenth century and early twentieth century and is now in the public domain. The characteristic deep mahogany crust is produced by the reaction of the beeswaxed copper mould with the high-sugar custard batter. Traditional copper moulds are used here; silicone moulds produce a lighter, less caramelised result.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter must be made at least 12 hours — and ideally 24 to 48 hours — before baking. It keeps refrigerated for up to 3 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, starting high (250°C for 10 minutes) then reducing to 190°C for 50 minutes. The initial blast sets the crust. Fan oven: 230°C reducing to 170°C.

glossaryTerms.definition: Food-grade beeswax used to coat the inside of copper canelé moulds before each bake. The wax creates the distinctive glossy, caramelised shell on the exterior. Applied melted with a pastry brush and left to set before the cold batter is poured in.

The process of bringing a cold ingredient gradually up to temperature by adding a small amount of hot liquid before combining fully. Here, the hot milk is poured into the egg mixture in a thin stream to prevent the eggs from scrambling.

content.text: Canelés require two things that most home bakers overlook: a very long batter rest (at least 24 hours in the fridge), and copper moulds coated with

before each bake. Silicone moulds work but produce a paler, less lacquered result. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; an oven thermometer is essential for a recipe where the first 10 minutes at 250°C are what builds the caramelised shell.

Bring the milk, butter, vanilla pod, and seeds to a simmer. Remove from heat and allow to cool to 80°C. Sift the flour, icing sugar, and salt into a large bowl. Whisk the whole eggs and egg yolks together, then pour over the flour. Using the

principle, pour the warm milk mixture in a thin stream over the flour and egg mixture, whisking constantly until smooth. Add the rum. The batter will be very liquid. Strain through a sieve, cover, and refrigerate for at least 12 hours and up to 48 hours.

Preheat the oven to 250°C conventional. Melt the beeswax in a small pan or in the microwave. Brush the inside of each copper mould very thoroughly with melted beeswax, getting into all the fluted grooves. Place the moulds on a baking tray and put in the preheating oven for 3 minutes to allow the wax to set against the hot metal, then remove. Allow to cool for 5 minutes.

Give the cold batter a thorough stir. Pour into the moulds to within 5 mm of the rim; the batter will rise initially then settle. Bake at 250°C for 10 minutes, then reduce to 190°C and bake for a further 45 to 55 minutes. The canelés are done when the exterior is a deep mahogany-brown, almost black at the flutes. Unmould immediately onto a rack; if left in the moulds the steam will soften the crust. Eat within a few hours of baking; the interior softens overnight.
